content.assessment: This compensatory approach leaves the selection age and track structure entirely intact, meaning the core mechanisms generating inequality of opportunity—early tracking (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01) and SES-skewed access even controlling for ability (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02)—remain fully in place. Compensatory funding may partially offset losses in disadvantaged schools from high-performer departure, but it does not change who gains access to the higher value-added selective tracks, nor does it address Roma segregation or geographic access disparities documented independently. The within-school sorting finding also suggests some benefit could be absorbed by persistent classroom-level sorting (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10).
opportunities: Could complement structural reforms (e.g., TP-live-t1 or TP-live-t3) by addressing resource disparities that would otherwise persist regardless of admission or tracking-age changes
strengths: Could improve absolute outcomes for students remaining in ordinary tracks, who are documented to show deteriorating relative performance after selection (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04)
Addresses school-level resourcing without requiring politically contentious structural change
Does not require legal or constitutional challenge risk associated with catchment/admission redesign
threats: Continued outflow of high performers and associated capitation losses under an unchanged selection mechanism could outpace compensatory funding effects, since selective-track advantage is documented to concentrate rather than diffuse (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-04)
Without structural change, the underlying capital-intensive pre-selection competition documented for disadvantaged schools continues unaddressed (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-03)
weaknesses: Does not alter the SES-skewed access mechanism to selective tracks, which persists even controlling for prior ability (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-02)
Does not address the early-tracking structural mechanism linked to inequality (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01)
Does not address Roma segregation, a distinct mechanism unrelated to school funding levels (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-06)
Persistent within-school SES sorting could absorb intended benefits of compensatory resourcing (F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-10)
